message:
refactor(writing): streamline CMA-ES article and fix visualization types

Major content revision of the CMA-ES optimization article to improve
clarity and reduce redundancy while maintaining technical depth.

cmaes-article.tsx:
- Condense explanatory content for better readability
- Improve section structure and visual hierarchy
- Streamline code examples and explanations
- Reduce overall article length while preserving key insights

cmaes-visualizations.tsx:
- Add explicit TypeScript type annotations for array map callbacks
- Fix implicit 'any' type errors in covariance matrix display
- Parameters: (row: number[], i: number) and (val: number, j: number)
- Improve type safety throughout visualization components

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

- ); - - if (explanation) { - return {content}; + return {content}; } return content; } @@ -105,14 +86,7 @@ function J({ t: string; children?: React.ReactNode; }) { - const termData = getJargon(t); - if (!termData) return <>{children}; - - return ( - - {children || termData.term} - - ); + return {children}; } // Section divider @@ -219,16 +193,13 @@ export function CMAESArticle() {

- If you live anywhere near modern machine learning, optimization almost automatically means gradients. Adam, Adafactor, Lion, SGD with warm restarts—pick your favorite flavor, they’re all dancing to the same tune: move parameters downhill along a gradient you can compute cheaply. -

-

- But there’s a big category of problems where the gradient either doesn’t exist, is meaningless, or is so expensive to approximate that you might as well not bother. In that world—what people call black-box optimization—my favorite tool by a wide margin is CMA-ES, the Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy. + In modern machine learning, optimization almost automatically implies gradients. Adam, Adafactor, Lion, and SGD are all variants of the same concept: moving parameters downhill along a cheaply computed gradient.

- CMA-ES is a Zero-Order optimizer. It doesn’t need to know the slope of the hill; it just needs to know the height at different points. It gives you a principled way to turn "I can only evaluate this thing" into "I can reliably walk toward a good solution." + However, many important problems exist where the gradient is either absent, meaningless, or too expensive to approximate. For these black-box optimization scenarios, CMA-ES (Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy) is a remarkably effective tool.

- This is an essay about why I like CMA-ES so much, how it works at an intuitive level, where it came from, and why I think it’s still wildly underused in the neural-net world. + CMA-ES is a Zero-Order optimizer. It operates without knowing the local slope of the landscape, relying instead on the height of the objective at various points. This approach provides a principled method for navigating toward optimal solutions using only function evaluations.

- 1. What CMA-ES Is (And Why You Should Care) + 1. The Search Mechanism

- At the highest possible level, CMA-ES does this: + CMA-ES maintains a Gaussian search distribution over the parameter space. It iteratively adapts the mean and covariance of this Gaussian to concentrate probability mass in regions where the objective performs well.

-
-

- It keeps a Gaussian search distribution over your parameter space and iteratively morphs that Gaussian—its mean and its covariance—to concentrate probability mass where the objective looks good. -

-

- Concretely, suppose you have some unknown function that you want to minimize. You tell CMA-ES: + Consider an unknown function that requires minimization. The algorithm is initialized with the dimension , an initial parameter guess, and a starting step scale.

-
    -
  • the dimension
    • -
  • an initial guess for the parameters (or just "center me in the middle of the search box")
    • -
  • an initial overall step scale
    • -
-

Then, repeatedly:

+

The process then repeats through four primary phases:

  1. 1.
    - Sampling: It samples a small population of candidate points from a multivariate normal: + Sampling: Candidate points are drawn from a multivariate normal distribution: - where is the current mean, is a scalar step-size, and is a full covariance matrix encoding the current "shape" of promising directions. + where is the current mean, is a scalar step-size, and is a covariance matrix defining the current search shape.
  2. 2. -
    - Evaluation: It evaluates your expensive black-box at each point. -
    +
    Evaluation: The black-box function is evaluated at each point.
  3. 3. -
    - Ranking: It ranks the samples by how good they were. -
    +
    Ranking: Samples are sorted by their objective performance.
  4. 4. -
    - Adaptation: It nudges , , and so that the next Gaussian puts more probability mass where the good samples came from and less where the bad ones came from. -
    +
    Adaptation: The parameters , , and are adjusted to increase the likelihood of sampling from high-performance regions in the next generation.
-

- Over time, the initially spherical Gaussian turns into a rotated, stretched ellipsoid that lines up with the valleys and ridges of your objective function. It’s implicitly learning something like the local Hessian without ever seeing a derivative. -

-

- Early on, samples are spread out, the covariance is mostly spherical, and the algorithm is "groping in the dark." As evidence accumulates about which directions lead to better values, the ellipsoid tilts, stretches, and shrinks, zooming in on promising regions. +

+ The initially spherical Gaussian gradually transforms into a rotated and stretched ellipsoid. This shape aligns with the valleys and ridges of the objective function, implicitly learning the local Hessian without calculating derivatives.

- 2. What It Means Not to Have a Gradient + 2. The Differentiability Gap

- When you first learn optimization, "no gradient" sounds like a minor annoyance. Just finite-difference it, right? Wrong. In the real world, the mapping from parameters to outcome is often a jagged, discontinuous landscape where derivatives are meaningless. + In many practical engineering settings, the mapping from parameters to outcomes is a jagged and discontinuous landscape. Traditional derivatives in these environments are often meaningless.

Airplane Wings

- Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations take hours on a cluster. Meshing and turbulence model switching create sharp discontinuities. One gradient step could take days of compute. + CFD simulations can take hours per run. Discontinuities arising from meshing and turbulence models make finite differences unreliable. A single gradient step might require days of computation.

Suspension Bridges

- Finite element models with mode-crossing and discrete material choices. Sharp changes when constraints flip from satisfied to violated. Gradient descent is helpless against brittle constraints. + Finite element models involve sharp changes as constraints flip between satisfied and violated states. Brittle constraints and mode-crossing make standard gradient descent ineffective.

@@ -353,66 +305,45 @@ export function CMAESArticle() {

Transformer Hyperparameters

-

- Layer counts, attention heads, and activation choices are inherently discrete. Training runs are ruinously expensive. Your evaluation budget is maybe a couple hundred points total. +

+ Architecture choices such as layer counts and attention heads are inherently discrete. The extreme cost of training runs often limits the total evaluation budget to a few hundred points.

200
-
Max Simulations
+
Typical Eval Budget

- You absolutely cannot afford to do "naive" grid search either. A modest grid with 10 values per dimension and 10 dimensions is simulations—ludicrous. But you can afford something like a few thousand simulations, if each one moves you in an intelligently chosen direction. + Naive grid search is equally impractical; a simple 10-dimensional grid with 10 values per axis requires simulations. CMA-ES provides a solution by efficiently moving through the search space using a manageable number of samples.

- {/* ========== 3. SLOW MOTION WALKTHROUGH ========== */} + {/* ========== 3. GRADIENT HIERARCHY ========== */}
-

- 3. CMA-ES in Slow Motion: Designing a Wing in 3D +

+ 3. The Gradient Hierarchy

-

- Let’s go back to the wing, but drastically simplify to keep things concrete. Suppose we compress all our design freedom into just three scalars: -

-
    -
  1. : normalized aspect ratio in
    2. -
  2. : normalized sweep angle in
    3. -
  3. : normalized airfoil family index in
    4. -
-

- Normalizing everything to roughly comparable ranges is important because CMA-ES’s initial Gaussian is isotropic. If one variable lives on a scale of 0.001 and another on 10, you’re already telling the algorithm that some directions are "long" and some "short" before it has seen any data. I like my ignorance to be symmetric. -

- -

The Gradient Hierarchy

- Let’s be clear: If you have a clean, cheap gradient, you should almost always use it. First-order methods like Adam or SGD are incredibly efficient because they know exactly which way is downhill. + Clear and inexpensive gradients should almost always be leveraged. First-order methods like Adam or SGD are highly efficient because they utilize the exact local slope.

- CMA-ES is for the world where the gradient is missing, expensive, or fake (numerical noise). The comparison below isn’t about replacing gradients; it’s about showing how CMA-ES provides a robust fallback when local slopes are too deceptive to trust. + CMA-ES is intended for scenarios where gradients are unavailable, computationally prohibitive, or obscured by numerical noise. It serves as a robust alternative when local slopes are too deceptive for reliable optimization.

-

- By Generation 5, something interesting happens. The covariance is no longer the identity. Its eigenvectors roughly align with directions in which the objective varies gently vs sharply. -

-

- In the wing case, you might find that changing aspect ratio and sweep together along some diagonal barely changes performance, but moving orthogonally is catastrophic. CMA-ES captures that by turning the sampling ellipsoid into a rotated cigar aligned with the benign direction. +

+ By the fifth generation, the covariance typically evolves beyond its initial state. Its eigenvectors align with directions where the objective varies gently, allowing the distribution to navigate narrow valleys effectively. This process approximates the local curvature without requiring a formal Hessian or explicit gradients.

-
-

- It’s approximating the local curvature of your objective without ever forming a Hessian and without relying on gradients. -

-

- The Invariance Superpower + The Invariance Property

- One reason CMA-ES is so robust is its Invariance properties. It is designed to be "coordinate-free." + CMA-ES maintains robustness through its Invariance properties, making it essentially "coordinate-free."

-
+

Translation & Rotation

- If you rotate your entire search space or move your starting point, CMA-ES will find the exact same path. It doesn’t care about the labels on your axes. + Rotating the search space or shifting the starting point does not alter the algorithm’s trajectory. It is independent of the axis orientation.

-
+

Rank-Based Scaling

- Because it only uses the rank of samples, it is invariant to any strictly increasing transform of the objective. Optimizing is the same as or . + Because selection relies solely on the rank of samples, the algorithm is invariant to monotonic transforms of the objective. Optimizing yields identical results to optimizing .

- This makes it one of the few "set and forget" optimizers. You don’t need to spend hours tuning the scales of your parameters or preprocessing your objective values. + This design minimizes the need for precise parameter scaling or objective preprocessing, making it a reliable default for diverse problems.

@@ -453,16 +384,16 @@ export function CMAESArticle() {

- Filtering the Noise + Stochastic Robustness

- In many engineering problems, your black-box isn’t a perfect math function. It’s a simulation with rounding errors, or a physical experiment with sensors. It is Stochastic—jittery and noisy. + Many engineering black-boxes are Stochastic, producing noisy feedback due to rounding errors or sensor jitter.

-

- Gradient-based methods often choke on noise because they rely on a single local estimate. CMA-ES uses its entire population to average out the jitter, seeing through the "fog" to find the underlying global structure. +

+ Gradient-based methods often struggle with noise because they depend on single-point estimates. CMA-ES leverages its entire population to filter out jitter, identifying the underlying global structure through the noise.

@@ -473,16 +404,16 @@ export function CMAESArticle() {

- Escaping the Local Trap + Escaping Local Minima

- Most real-world landscapes aren’t simple bowls. They are Multimodal—full of jagged peaks and deceptive Local Minima that can trap a small population. CMA-ES solves this with a "Restart with Increasing Population" strategy (IPOP). + Real-world landscapes are frequently Multimodal, containing deceptive Local Minima. CMA-ES addresses this through a "Restart with Increasing Population" strategy (IPOP).

- If progress stalls, the algorithm "explodes" its search radius and sends out a much larger army of scouts. This transition from local exploitation to global exploration is what allows CMA-ES to find the deepest valley in a massive mountain range. + When progress stalls, the algorithm increases its search radius and expands the population size. This shift from local refinement to global exploration enables CMA-ES to locate the global optimum in vast and complex search spaces.

@@ -493,31 +424,31 @@ export function CMAESArticle() {

- 4. Where CMA-ES Came From + 4. Historical Context

- CMA-ES didn’t appear out of nowhere. It’s the product of several converging lines of thought. + CMA-ES is the culmination of several historical lines of optimization research.

Evolution Strategies

- While classical genetic algorithms focused on bitstrings and crossover, the evolution strategies community in Germany emphasized continuous search spaces and Gaussian mutations. CMA-ES, introduced by Hansen & Ostermeier in the mid-1990s, pushed this to its logical conclusion: adapt a full covariance matrix. + In contrast to genetic algorithms that emphasized bitstrings and crossover, the German evolution strategies community focused on continuous search spaces and Gaussian mutations. CMA-ES, introduced in the mid-1990s, extended this by adapting a full covariance matrix.

Information Geometry

- If you think of CMA-ES as moving a distribution in the manifold of Gaussians, the right answer is the natural gradient: perform steepest ascent with respect to the Fisher information metric. Akimoto et al. showed that the core CMA-ES update is exactly a sampled natural-gradient step. + Viewed through the lens of information geometry, the core CMA-ES update is a natural gradient step on the manifold of multivariate normals. This provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for its adaptive behavior.

Kriging & Gaussian Processes

- CMA-ES shares a "Gaussian + covariance + unbiasedness" mindset with Kriging (GP regression). While Kriging models the function itself, CMA-ES uses the Gaussian as a proposal distribution to sample points with low objective values. + CMA-ES shares a common philosophical basis with Kriging (Gaussian-process regression). While Kriging models the unknown function itself, CMA-ES uses its Gaussian model as a proposal distribution to explore regions of low objective values.

@@ -530,89 +461,86 @@ export function CMAESArticle() {

- 5. Two Optimization Worlds That Barely Talk + 5. Disciplinary Silos

- One of the weird sociological facts about CMA-ES is that it lives, culturally, in a different universe from most deep-learning folks. + Optimization research is often divided into distinct communities that rarely interact.

-
+

- GECCO World + GECCO

- The evolutionary computation community. They talk about EDAs, surrogate models, and CMA-ES as the primary language of optimization. + The evolutionary computation community focuses on EDAs, surrogate models, and derivative-free methods as the primary language of optimization.

-
+

- NeurIPS World + NeurIPS

-

- Deep learning practitioners. They know backprop and SGD. They know Bayesian optimization, but evolutionary computation feels like a different discipline. +

+ The deep learning community primarily relies on backpropagation and SGD variants. While they utilize Bayesian optimization, evolutionary computation is often treated as a separate discipline.

- This siloing is mostly historical accident. There’s an interesting overlap between how CMA-ES thinks about learning a distribution and how LeCun’s Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA) think about predicting missing parts of an image. + Bridging these worlds reveals conceptual overlaps, such as the relationship between CMA-ES and architectures like LeCun’s Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA).

- {/* ========== 6. DEEP LEARNING HYBRIDS ========== */} + {/* ========== 6. CREATIVE SYSTEMS ========== */}

- 6. CMA-ES + Deep Learning + Creative Systems + 6. Creative System Optimization

- The irony is that CMA-ES is an incredibly natural companion to modern DL systems once you stop insisting that everything be end-to-end differentiable. + CMA-ES is particularly useful for tuning systems where end-to-end differentiability is not feasible.

-

Tuning Continuous Cellular Automata

-

- Imagine designing a continuous CA with interesting visual dynamics. You want to score patterns along dimensions like complexity, persistence, or "aesthetic interest." -

+

Continuous Cellular Automata

- There are no gradients that connect these high-level properties to underlying parameters. But you can map the parameters into a unit hypercube and let CMA-ES search. It doesn’t understand "visual interest"; it just knows which vectors yield higher scores and deforms its Gaussian to find more of them. + Designing a continuous CA with specific visual dynamics—such as complexity or coherence—requires a scalar fitness function. Because gradients between these high-level properties and kernel weights do not exist, CMA-ES serves as an automated experimental collaborator, identifying parameter settings that produce intended dynamics.

-
-

The Automated Experimental Collaborator

+
+

Automated Collaboration

- You write the simulator and the scoring function; CMA-ES does the boring part of exploring the parameter space. It ends up with settings you’d never have guessed by hand-tuning. + By deforming its search distribution based on empirical results, the algorithm identifies configurations that would be difficult to discover through manual tuning.

- {/* ========== LIVE BENCHMARKS ========== */} + {/* ========== IMPLEMENTATIONS ========== */}

- 7. Live Benchmarks & Implementations + 7. Implementations

-

- Watch the algorithm crawl its way through different landscapes while adapting its own notion of "where the promising valleys live." +

+ Watch the algorithm navigate different landscapes in real-time below.

-

Two Implementations I Built

+

Personal Rust Implementations

-
-

wasm_cmaes

-

Rust CMA-ES compiled to WebAssembly, wrapped in a clean JS/TS API. Features SIMD-accelerated vector ops and Rayon parallelism inside the WASM module.

- GitHub Repo +
+

wasm_cmaes

+

Rust CMA-ES compiled to WebAssembly, featuring SIMD-accelerated vector operations and internal Rayon parallelism.

+ Source Code
-
-

fast_cmaes

-

Hyper-optimized Rust core with a first-class Python experience. SIMD, Rayon, deterministic seeds, and a Rich-powered TUI for terminal monitoring.

- GitHub Repo +
+

fast_cmaes

+

A high-performance Rust core with Python bindings, featuring vectorized objectives and a Rich-powered TUI.

+ Source Code

- Based on Nikolaus Hansen’s 2024 tutorial. + Notes derived from Nikolaus Hansen’s 2024 tutorial.

-

The Search Distribution as Central Object

+

The Search Distribution

- You’re not optimizing directly; you’re optimizing a probability distribution over . All adaptation happens in distribution space . + Optimization is conducted over a probability distribution , rather than directly in the parameter space.

-

Invariance as Design Principle

-

- CMA-ES is designed for rank-based invariance: it only uses the ordering of fitness values, making it invariant to any strictly increasing transform of . It is also invariant under rigid linear transforms of the search space. +

Invariance Properties

+

+ The algorithm is designed for rank-based invariance, ensuring consistent behavior under strictly increasing transforms of the objective function and rigid linear transforms of the search space.

-

Step-size Control (CSA)

+

Adaptive Step-size Control

- The Cumulative Step-size Adaptation compares the evolution path length to what you’d expect from a random walk. If the path is "too long," it means we’re consistently moving in a correlated direction → increase . + Cumulative Step-size Adaptation (CSA) increases the step scale when the evolution path indicates consistent directional progress.
